title: Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement Learning
obj: Students will be able to distinguish the three main ML paradigms, identify examples of each, and select the right paradigm for a given problem.
content: Supervised learning
train on labeled data (input + correct output); examples: spam classification, house-price regression
Regression vs. classification
regression predicts numbers (price, age); classification predicts categories (spam/not, dog/cat)
Unsupervised learning
no labels — the model finds structure; examples: clustering customers, dimensionality reduction
Clustering
k-means, hierarchical — group similar items; used in market segmentation
Reinforcement learning
agent takes actions in an environment, gets rewards/penalties; used in games, robotics, ads
How to choose
have labels + predict a known outcome → supervised; exploring data without labels → unsupervised; sequential decisions with rewards → RL
assess: Classify 6 problem statements by paradigm and justify
Design: describe a supervised, an unsupervised, and an RL approach to the same problem (e.g., music recommendation)
Short answer: what's the fundamental data requirement of supervised learning that unsupervised doesn't need?

title: Training and Evaluating ML Models
obj: Students will be able to train a simple classifier, evaluate its performance with appropriate metrics, and describe what worked and what didn't.
content: Training loop concept
show the model examples, compute loss, update parameters, repeat — supervised learning in one sentence
Accuracy
% correct — useful when classes are balanced; misleading when imbalanced
Precision and recall
precision = of predicted positives, how many were right; recall = of actual positives, how many were found
Confusion matrix
true/false × positive/negative grid; the ground truth of 'what's happening with my model'
Overfitting vs underfitting
overfitting: memorizes training data; underfitting: doesn't learn enough. Use val set to detect
Describing results
report metric, caveats, data size, failure cases — not just 'my model got 95% accuracy'
assess: Lab: train a sklearn LogisticRegression on a small dataset and report accuracy + confusion matrix
Interpret: given a confusion matrix, compute precision and recall
Short answer: why is training-set accuracy a dangerous metric?

title: Investigating How Daily-Use AI Works
obj: Students will be able to investigate a daily-use AI product and determine which type(s) of AI it relies on.
content: Evidence-gathering
look at the product's documentation, academic papers from the team, reverse-engineer from behavior
Pattern: social media
recommender systems = supervised + RL; content moderation = supervised CV + NLP
Pattern: voice assistants
wake word = CV-on-audio; command understanding = NLP; response = GenAI + retrieval
Pattern: smartphones
camera scene detection = CV; autocorrect = NLP; battery prediction = regression
Pattern: shopping
product recommendations = collaborative filtering (unsupervised-ish); price prediction = regression
Journalistic skills
read the Terms of Service, privacy policy, and help pages — often tells you what AI they use
assess: Investigate: pick 3 apps you use daily and identify the AI types they likely use, with evidence
Research: read one major platform's AI transparency page and summarize
Short answer: why might a company be coy about exactly what AI they're using?

title: AI Bias, Perception, Privacy, and Accuracy
obj: Students will be able to define and identify bias, perception, privacy, and accuracy issues in AI systems.
content: Bias in AI
systematic unfairness — dataset bias, historical bias, measurement bias, selection bias
Perception
how AI 'sees' the world can differ from reality (e.g., facial-recognition error rates higher for some groups)
Privacy
what info does the AI collect, store, share? can it re-identify anonymized data?
Accuracy
how often is the model right? breaks down by group — global accuracy can hide disparities
Real examples
COMPAS recidivism scores, Amazon résumé screener, facial recognition audits
assess: Match: given 4 real AI incidents, identify which of (bias/perception/privacy/accuracy) each illustrates
Research: find one 2024+ story about one of these issues and summarize
Short answer: why can a model be 'accurate overall' and still be unfair?

title: Exploring Bias Hands-On with Teachable Machine
obj: Students will be able to deliberately create and then detect bias in a no-code ML model, and propose remediation.
content: Design the experiment
build a 2-class classifier; deliberately under-sample one class, or use unrepresentative photos
Test on held-out examples
use photos not seen during training, including examples the model should classify but was underexposed to
Observe bias
confidence scores drop or flip for underrepresented group — the model learned 'what's common'
Remediation options
add more training samples for the minority class; remove the confounding variable; augment with varied poses/lighting
Document findings
record accuracy per class, not just overall; write up what you saw and why
assess: Lab: build a biased 2-class classifier intentionally; test and record per-class accuracy
Remediate: rebalance training data and re-test; compare before/after accuracy
Short answer: how does this small experiment connect to the real-world facial-recognition audits?

title: AI Ethics and Philosophy
obj: Students will be able to describe major ethical frameworks and apply them to AI use cases.
content: Consequentialism
judge actions by outcomes — how much net good or harm did the AI cause?
Deontology
judge by duties and rules — are we violating rights regardless of outcomes? (e.g., privacy, consent)
Virtue ethics
what would a virtuous person/organization do? humility, honesty, care
Justice and fairness
Rawls' veil of ignorance; design AI as if you might be any affected group
Applied AI ethics
the IEEE Ethically Aligned Design, the EU AI Act principles, Partnership on AI's guidelines
Ethics is ongoing
not a checkbox — require regular audits, diverse teams, and stakeholder input
assess: Apply: analyze one AI deployment (e.g., predictive policing) through each of the 4 frameworks
Discuss: case study — what should a company do when its AI detects a minor crime committed by its CEO?
Short answer: how does virtue ethics differ from consequentialism in practice?

title: Legal and Regulatory Landscape of AI
obj: Students will be able to describe key laws, regulations, and legal policies that govern AI systems.
content: EU AI Act
tiered risk-based rules in force; bans on certain uses; strict requirements for high-risk systems
US executive orders
recent orders on AI safety, reporting requirements, federal procurement
State-level laws
California, Colorado, Illinois — biometric privacy, deepfake rules, employment-AI disclosures
Copyright and AI
ongoing lawsuits over training data; fair use disputes; the Getty vs Stability AI case
Liability
when AI causes harm, who is responsible — developer, deployer, user? courts are still deciding
Sector-specific rules
HIPAA for health AI, FERPA for education AI, FCRA for credit AI
assess: Research: summarize one recent AI lawsuit in 3 sentences
Apply: for a hypothetical school-grading AI, identify 3 laws that would govern it
Short answer: why are regulators having a hard time keeping up with AI?

title: AI for Good Organizations
obj: Students will be able to identify organizations working on beneficial and safe AI and describe one current project at each.
content: AI for Good Foundation
NGO network focused on AI for humanitarian and sustainable development goals
Center for Human-Compatible AI
UC Berkeley research center on aligning AI with human values
Future of Life Institute
grants, papers, and advocacy on long-term AI safety and existential risk
Partnership on AI
industry consortium on responsible AI practices
Local orgs
school-level clubs, hackathons, and student-led AI-for-good chapters
How to get involved
internship, open-source contribution, hackathon, volunteering on data-collection or verification
assess: Research: pick one organization and summarize one current project
Design: pitch an 'AI for Good' project for your school or community
Short answer: why is independent, non-corporate AI research important for safety?
